message.content: Read /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/agent_prompts/hld_session_builder.md IN FULL. This is the agent recipe for building ONE complete HLD interview session. Extract: the end-to-end recipe steps in order; whether HLD generation writes any workspace files or runs any commands (or is markdown-only); the EXACT conversation-JSON contract it documents for hld mode (session_id form with :hld, mode field, the messages[]/transcript[]/blocks[] shape, and whether any tool blocks like write_file are used); and every CRITICAL/gotcha note. If the file references old /Users/anshullkgarg/... paths, note that the real project root is /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora. Quote field names verbatim.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll read the HLD session builder recipe file in full.

content.content:
1	# HLD Session Builder — Agent Prompt
2	
3	> **Model:** `claude-opus-4-8`
4	> **How to run:** Open Claude Code CLI in your project root, switch model with `/model claude-opus-4-8`, then type the problem statement followed by the contents of this file. OR use the Agent tool from within Claude Code with `model: "opus"` and paste this entire file as the prompt.
5	
6	You are building a **complete, ready-to-open HLD (High Level Design / System Design) interview session** for the Algora frontend. When you are done, the user must be able to open it at `https://localhost:8002/?s=<SESSION_ID>:hld` and see the full design.
7	
8	---
9	
10	## What you must produce
11	
12	Given a system design problem (e.g. "Design Twitter", "Design an E-Commerce Platform", "Design a URL Shortener"), you will:
13	
14	1. **Generate the full §1–§11 HLD response** as markdown.
15	2. **Write the conversation JSON** so the frontend can open the session.
16	
17	HLD sessions do NOT have Python workspace files — they are entirely prose, tables, and diagrams.
18	
19	---
20	
21	## The HLD System Prompt
22	
23	Before generating anything, read the full HLD system prompt from:
24	```
25	/Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/system_design_prompt.md
26	```
27	This is the exact prompt the AI uses in production. Your output must match every section it describes (§1 through §11).
28	
29	---
30	
31	## Step 1 — Pick a session ID
32	
33	Use a fresh UUID4 for the session. The session ID format is:
34	```
35	<uuid>:hld
36	```
37	Example: `a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890:hld`
38	
39	The **slug** (used for file paths) is the session ID with every non-alphanumeric character except `-` and `_` replaced by `_`:
40	```python
41	import re
42	slug = re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9_-]', '_', session_id)
43	# "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890:hld" → "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890_hld"
44	```
45	
46	---
47	
48	## Step 2 — Generate the full §1–§11 HLD response
49	
50	Using the system prompt from `system_design_prompt.md`, generate the complete HLD response as markdown. This is what the user will READ — it must be fully expanded, no shortcuts.
51	
52	### Required sections (all 11):
53	
54	**§1 — Requirements**
55	- Bullet each functional requirement on its own line
56	- Layman parenthetical after every requirement: `(what this means in plain English)`
57	- "Why it's hard" line per requirement
58	- Every NFR must have a "What breaks without it" line
59	
60	**§2 — Clarifying Questions**
61	- 6–10 questions per category (scale, consistency, latency)
62	- Every question has: default assumption + design fork (If YES → ..., If NO → ...)
63	- 🗣️ plain-words line showing how to say it aloud in the interview
64	
65	**§3 — Capacity Estimation**
66	- Full derivation chains showing every intermediate step
67	- Every acronym/term glossed on first use (QPS = Queries Per Second, etc.)
68	- Final summary table: metric, value, raw calculation
69	
70	**§4 — Data Model**
71	- Each entity: one-line role, table of fields with types
72	- 🎙️ Spoken intro: "I'd start by talking about the key entities and why each one exists"
73	- Simple analogy per important entity (e.g. "think of this like a spreadsheet row")
74	- Interviewer probe Q per entity
75	
76	**§5 — API Design**
77	- Table of key endpoints with method, path, auth
78	- Per endpoint: key request fields + why, key response fields + why
79	- 🗣️ how to narrate each endpoint choice aloud
80	
81	**§6 — High-Level Architecture**
82	- Mermaid diagram (wrapped in ` ```mermaid `)
83	- Decision table: component → options considered → chosen → why → 🗣️ plain words → what we rejected
84	- Subgraph block per major flow showing the components involved in that flow
85	- 4-part step narration for at least 2 critical flows: what user does → what hits first → what happens in the middle → what responds
86	
87	**§7 — Data Model & Storage**
88	- 3-part decision card per storage decision:
89	  - Plain definition: "What is this: ..."
90	  - What we rejected: "We considered X but ..."
91	  - How to say it: 🗣️ "In the interview I'd say ..."
92	- Include: primary DB choice, cache layer, blob/object storage, search index if relevant
93	
94	**§8 — Deep Dives**
95	- 6-part tier structure per deep dive topic:
96	  1. What the previous tier got wrong (why we need this component)
97	  2. How this tier fixes it
98	  3. Decision math (numbers that justify the choice)
99	  4. Failure modes (what can go wrong here)
100	  5. Failure matrix: failure → impact → mitigation
101	  6. 🗣️ How to narrate this to the interviewer
102	- Cover at least: caching strategy, database sharding/replication, async processing, CDN/global distribution
103	
104	**§9 — Reliability & Fault Tolerance**
105	- Define RPO and RTO in plain English first, then state target values
106	- 4 sub-sections:
107	  1. Single points of failure + how we eliminate them
108	  2. Replication strategy (sync vs async — when and why)
109	  3. Circuit breakers, retries, backoff
110	  4. Disaster recovery: backup frequency, restore process, multi-region plan
111	
112	**§10 — Trade-off Ledger**
113	- 5-part decision card per major trade-off:
114	  1. The choice made
115	  2. What we gave up
116	  3. Why we made this trade
117	  4. When this reverses (what would make us choose differently)
118	  5. 🗣️ How to say it in the interview
119	- Cover at least: consistency vs availability, SQL vs NoSQL, sync vs async, monolith vs microservices
120	
121	**§11 — Likely Interviewer Questions**
122	- ALL 7 question domains required:
123	  1. Core algorithm / data structure choices
124	  2. Failure handling (what happens when X goes down)
125	  3. Scale / hot-spot handling
126	  4. Consistency and data guarantees
127	  5. Security and access control
128	  6. Cost optimization
129	  7. Extensibility / future requirements
130	- Per question: 4-part answer format:
131	  - One-line direct answer
132	  - Supporting detail (2-3 sentences)
133	  - Trade-off acknowledgement
134	  - 🗣️ Interview-ready phrasing
135	- Close with a 60-second verbal summary template the user can memorize
136	
137	---
138	
139	## Step 3 — Write the conversation JSON
140	
141	File path:
142	```
143	/Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/data/conversations/<slug>.json
144	```
145	
146	### JSON structure:
147	```json
148	{
149	  "session_id": "<uuid>:hld",
150	  "mode": "hld",
151	  "title": "Design <System Name>",
152	  "created_at": <unix timestamp>,
153	  "updated_at": <unix timestamp + 120>,
154	  "messages": [],
155	  "transcript": [
156	    {
157	      "role": "user",
158	      "text": "Design <System Name>",
159	      "images": 0
160	    },
161	    {
162	      "role": "assistant",
163	      "thinking": "",
164	      "blocks": [
165	        {
166	          "k": "text",
167	          "md": "<THE FULL §1-§11 MARKDOWN RESPONSE>"
168	        }
169	      ],
170	      "usage": {
171	        "input_tokens": 8192,
172	        "output_tokens": 18000,
173	        "cache_read_input_tokens": 24000,
174	        "cache_creation_input_tokens": 0
175	      }
176	    }
177	  ]
178	}
179	```
180	
181	### Important slug rule:
182	```python
183	import re
184	
185	session_id = "<uuid>:hld"
186	slug = re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9_-]', '_', session_id)
187	# slug == "<uuid>_hld"       ← filename (underscore)
188	# session_id == "<uuid>:hld" ← inside JSON (colon)
189	```
190	
191	Write the JSON using Python:
192	```python
193	import json, pathlib, time, uuid as _uuid
194	
195	session_id = f"{_uuid.uuid4()}:hld"
196	slug = re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9_-]', '_', session_id)
197	now = int(time.time())
198	
199	data = {
200	    "session_id": session_id,
201	    "mode": "hld",
202	    "title": "Design <System Name>",
203	    "created_at": now,
204	    "updated_at": now + 120,
205	    "messages": [],
206	    "transcript": [
207	        {
208	            "role": "user",
209	            "text": "Design <System Name>",
210	            "images": 0
211	        },
212	        {
213	            "role": "assistant",
214	            "thinking": "",
215	            "blocks": [
216	                {
217	                    "k": "text",
218	                    "md": FULL_MARKDOWN_RESPONSE
219	                }
220	            ],
221	            "usage": {
222	                "input_tokens": 8192,
223	                "output_tokens": 18000,
224	                "cache_read_input_tokens": 24000,
225	                "cache_creation_input_tokens": 0
226	            }
227	        }
228	    ]
229	}
230	
231	out = pathlib.Path('/Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/data/conversations') / f'{slug}.json'
232	out.write_text(json.dumps(data, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2), encoding='utf-8')
233	print(f"Written: {out}")
234	print(f"Open at: https://localhost:8002/?s={session_id}")
235	```
236	
237	---
238	
239	## Step 4 — Verify
240	
241	```bash
242	cd /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt
243	python3 -c "
244	import re, json, pathlib
245	sid = '<uuid>:hld'
246	slug = re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9_-]', '_', sid)
247	p = pathlib.Path('data/conversations') / f'{slug}.json'
248	assert p.exists(), f'Missing: {p}'
249	d = json.loads(p.read_text())
250	assert d['session_id'] == sid, 'session_id mismatch'
251	assert d['mode'] == 'hld'
252	assert d['transcript'][1]['blocks'][0]['k'] == 'text'
253	assert len(d['transcript'][1]['blocks'][0]['md']) > 10000, 'Response too short'
254	print('OK')
255	print(f'Open: https://localhost:8002/?s={sid}')
256	"
257	```
258	
259	---
260	
261	## Quality checklist before finishing
262	
263	- [ ] All 11 sections are present and fully expanded (not abbreviated)
264	- [ ] §1: every requirement has layman explanation + "why it's hard"
265	- [ ] §1: every NFR has "what breaks without it"
266	- [ ] §2: every question has default + design fork (If YES / If NO)
267	- [ ] §3: full derivation chains, all terms glossed
268	- [ ] §4: analogy + interviewer probe per entity
269	- [ ] §5: request/response fields with WHY per endpoint
270	- [ ] §6: Mermaid diagram + 5-column decision table + 4-part step narration for ≥2 flows
271	- [ ] §7: 3-part cards (what is it, what we rejected, how to say it)
272	- [ ] §8: 6-part tier structure, failure matrix per deep dive
273	- [ ] §9: RPO/RTO defined in plain English before use
274	- [ ] §10: 5-part cards with "when this reverses"
275	- [ ] §11: all 7 question domains + 4-part answers + 60-second summary template
276	- [ ] Conversation JSON exists at `data/conversations/<slug>.json`
277	- [ ] `session_id` inside JSON uses `:hld` (colon, not underscore)
278	- [ ] Slug in filename uses `_hld` (colon → underscore)
279	- [ ] Markdown in the text block is > 10,000 characters (must be thorough)
280

content.text: Confirmed. The real project root is `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora` and the paths translate accordingly. The `system_design_prompt.md` and `data/conversations/` both exist there. I have everything I need.

recipeSteps: Step 0 (prerequisite): Read the full HLD system prompt from system_design_prompt.md (recipe says /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/system_design_prompt.md; real path is /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/system_design_prompt.md) -- it is the exact production prompt and the output must match every section it describes, ss1 through ss11.

Step 1 -- Pick a session ID: use a fresh UUID4. Session ID format is '<uuid>:hld'. Example: 'a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890:hld'. Compute the slug = re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9_-]', '_', session_id), which turns ':hld' into '_hld' (used for the file path).

Step 2 -- Generate the full ss1-ss11 HLD response as markdown using the system prompt. This is what the user reads and must be fully expanded with no shortcuts. All 11 required sections: ss1 Requirements (layman parenthetical + 'why it's hard' per requirement, 'what breaks without it' per NFR); ss2 Clarifying Questions (6-10 per category, default assumption + design fork If YES/If NO, plain-words speaker line); ss3 Capacity Estimation (full derivation chains, every acronym glossed e.g. QPS = Queries Per Second, final summary table metric/value/raw calc); ss4 Data Model (entity role, field table with types, spoken intro, analogy, interviewer probe per entity); ss5 API Design (endpoint table method/path/auth, request+response fields with WHY, narration per endpoint); ss6 High-Level Architecture (mermaid diagram in ```mermaid fence, 5-column decision table component/options/chosen/why/plain-words/rejected, subgraph per flow, 4-part step narration for >=2 flows); ss7 Data Model & Storage (3-part decision card: what is this / what we rejected / how to say it; cover primary DB, cache, blob/object storage, search index if relevant); ss8 Deep Dives (6-part tier structure: what prev tier got wrong / how this fixes it / decision math / failure modes / failure matrix failure->impact->mitigation / narration; cover caching, sharding/replication, async processing, CDN/global); ss9 Reliability & Fault Tolerance (define RPO and RTO in plain English before stating targets; 4 subsections: SPOFs+elimination, sync vs async replication, circuit breakers/retries/backoff, disaster recovery); ss10 Trade-off Ledger (5-part card: choice / what gave up / why / when this reverses / how to say it; cover consistency vs availability, SQL vs NoSQL, sync vs async, monolith vs microservices); ss11 Likely Interviewer Questions (all 7 domains: algorithm/DS, failure handling, scale/hot-spot, consistency, security, cost, extensibility; 4-part answer per question: one-line answer / supporting detail / trade-off ack / interview phrasing; close with 60-second verbal summary template).

Step 3 -- Write the conversation JSON to data/conversations/<slug>.json (recipe path /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/data/conversations/<slug>.json; real path /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/data/conversations/<slug>.json). Filename uses <uuid>_hld.json (underscore); session_id inside JSON uses <uuid>:hld (colon). Recipe gives a Python snippet using json/pathlib/time/uuid that writes json.dumps(data, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2) encoded utf-8.

Step 4 -- Verify with the provided Python assertion script: assert file exists, d['session_id'] == sid, d['mode'] == 'hld', d['transcript'][1]['blocks'][0]['k'] == 'text', and len(d['transcript'][1]['blocks'][0]['md']) > 10000. Prints 'OK' and the open URL. Final open URL: https://localhost:8002/?s=<session_id> (i.e. <uuid>:hld).

Final -- Run the full quality checklist (16 items) confirming all 11 sections present/expanded and each section's required sub-structure, the JSON exists, session_id uses :hld, the slug filename uses _hld, and the markdown text block is > 10,000 characters.
input.fileOrWorkspaceNeeds: HLD generation is essentially markdown-only for the design content; it writes exactly ONE workspace file: the conversation JSON at data/conversations/<slug>.json. The recipe explicitly states 'HLD sessions do NOT have Python workspace files -- they are entirely prose, tables, and diagrams.' No code is executed/run as part of the deliverable -- the only commands are (a) the Python snippet in Step 3 that writes the JSON file, and (b) the Step 4 Python verification script that reads/asserts the JSON. There is no app build, no test run, no Python workspace/solution files. The system_design_prompt.md is only read, not written.
input.jsonContract: session_id form: '<uuid>:hld' (colon) inside the JSON; the on-disk filename uses the slug '<uuid>_hld.json' (colon replaced by underscore via re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9_-]', '_', session_id)). Top-level JSON object fields (verbatim): 'session_id' (= '<uuid>:hld'), 'mode' (= 'hld'), 'title' (= 'Design <System Name>'), 'created_at' (unix timestamp int), 'updated_at' (created_at + 120), 'messages' (= [] -- empty array), 'transcript' (array). NOTE: there is BOTH a 'messages' key (always empty []) AND a 'transcript' key; the content lives in 'transcript'. transcript[] holds exactly two entries: [0] the user turn = {'role':'user','text':'Design <System Name>','images':0}; [1] the assistant turn = {'role':'assistant','thinking':'' (empty string), 'blocks':[ {'k':'text','md':'<THE FULL ss1-ss11 MARKDOWN RESPONSE>'} ], 'usage':{'input_tokens':8192,'output_tokens':18000,'cache_read_input_tokens':24000,'cache_creation_input_tokens':0}}. blocks[] uses a single block with key 'k' = 'text' and key 'md' holding the entire markdown response. NO tool blocks (no write_file, no code/exec blocks) are used anywhere in hld mode -- only the single {'k':'text','md':...} block. Written via json.dumps(data, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2).
gotchas: PATH MISMATCH: the recipe hardcodes old paths under /Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/ (for system_design_prompt.md, data/conversations/, and the cd in Step 4). The real project root is /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora. Both /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/system_design_prompt.md and /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/data/conversations/ exist and are confirmed -- use the gbang paths.

COLON vs UNDERSCORE is the central gotcha and is called out twice: session_id INSIDE the JSON must use ':hld' (colon); the FILENAME slug must use '_hld' (colon -> underscore). Mixing these breaks session opening.
The slug is computed with re.sub(r'[^A-Za-z0-9_-]', '_', session_id) -- every non-alphanumeric char except '-' and '_' becomes '_'.
The markdown 'md' block MUST be > 10,000 characters; the Step 4 verifier asserts len(...) > 10000 and the checklist restates 'must be thorough'. Abbreviated/short responses fail.

ALL 11 sections must be present and FULLY EXPANDED (not abbreviated) -- the checklist enforces per-section structure (e.g. ss6 needs a ```mermaid``` diagram + 5-column decision table + 4-part narration for >=2 flows; ss9 must define RPO/RTO in plain English BEFORE giving target values; ss11 must cover all 7 question domains and end with a 60-second verbal summary template).
The JSON must contain BOTH an empty 'messages': [] array AND the populated 'transcript' array -- don't drop 'messages'.

Emoji markers are part of the spec the response must include (speaker emoji for spoken/narration lines, mic emoji for spoken intros in ss4). Write JSON with ensure_ascii=False so they survive, indent=2.

Must read system_design_prompt.md FIRST -- it is described as 'the exact prompt the AI uses in production' and the output must match every section (ss1-ss11) it describes. Do not generate from memory.
Final open URL uses the colon form: https://localhost:8002/?s=<uuid>:hld (port 8002, https, localhost).
assistant turn fields 'thinking' is an empty string '' (not null/omitted), and 'usage' carries fixed placeholder token counts (input 8192, output 18000, cache_read 24000, cache_creation 0).

content.text:
I have read the full HLD session builder recipe and verified the real paths. The structured output above captures the complete recipe, file/workspace needs, the exact conversation-JSON contract, and all gotchas.

Key findings:
- **File:** `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/agent_prompts/hld_session_builder.md`
- **Path correction:** The recipe hardcodes old `/Users/anshullkgarg/Desktop/projects/claude-gpt/` paths. The real root is `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora`. Confirmed both `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/system_design_prompt.md` and `/Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/data/conversations/` exist (the latter already holds many `*_hld.json` sessions).
- **Workspace writes:** Markdown-only design content; writes exactly one file (the conversation JSON at `data/conversations/<slug>.json`). No code/tests run beyond the Python that writes the JSON and the Python verifier.
- **JSON contract:** `session_id` = `<uuid>:hld` (colon) in JSON; filename slug = `<uuid>_hld.json` (underscore). Top-level keys: `session_id`, `mode` (`hld`), `title`, `created_at`, `updated_at`, empty `messages: []`, and `transcript[]` of two turns. The assistant turn carries a single `blocks` entry `{"k":"text","md": <full markdown>}` — no tool blocks (no `write_file`).
